Apple___Apple_scab.description: Apple scab is a fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis. It appears as olive-green to brown spots on leaves and fruit, eventually turning dark and scabby.
Apple___Apple_scab.treatment: Apply fungicides such as captan or myclobutanil during early spring. Remove and destroy infected leaves and fruit.
Apple___Apple_scab.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, ensure good air circulation, and avoid overhead watering.

Apple___Black_rot.description: Black rot is caused by the fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa. It causes circular brown lesions on leaves and rotting of fruit starting from the calyx end.
Apple___Black_rot.treatment: Prune out dead or infected wood. Apply copper-based fungicides. Remove mummified fruit from the tree.
Apple___Black_rot.prevention: Maintain tree health, remove dead wood promptly, and apply dormant sprays in early spring.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: Cedar apple rust is caused by Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. It produces bright orange-yellow spots on leaves and causes premature defoliation.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.treatment: Apply fungicides containing myclobutanil or triadimefon when leaves emerge. Remove nearby juniper trees if possible.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.prevention: Plant rust-resistant apple varieties and remove cedar or juniper trees near orchards.

Apple___healthy.description: The apple leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Apple___healthy.prevention: Continue regular monitoring, proper fertilisation, and good orchard hygiene.

Blueberry___healthy.description: The blueberry leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Blueberry___healthy.prevention: Maintain soil acidity between pH 4.5-5.5 and ensure adequate drainage.

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on cherry appears as white powdery coating on leaves, shoots, and fruit. It stunts growth and reduces fruit quality.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.treatment: Apply sulphur-based fungicides or potassium bicarbonate. Remove heavily infected shoots.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Ensure good air circulation, avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation, and plant in sunny locations.

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.description: The cherry leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.prevention: Monitor regularly and maintain good orchard sanitation.

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: Gray leaf spot is a fungal disease causing rectangular grey to tan lesions on corn leaves, running parallel to leaf veins. Severe infections cause significant yield loss.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.treatment: Apply foliar fungicides containing strobilurins or triazoles. Rotate crops to reduce inoculum.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.prevention: Use resistant hybrids, rotate with non-host crops, and reduce crop residue on the soil surface.

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Common rust produces small, round to elongated cinnamon-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces. Severe infections turn leaves yellow and reduce photosynthesis.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.treatment: Apply fungicides containing triazoles or strobilurins at early rust detection.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.prevention: Plant resistant hybrids and monitor fields regularly during humid weather.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Northern leaf blight causes long, elliptical grey-green lesions on corn leaves. Lesions turn tan with dark borders as they mature, reducing yield significantly.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.treatment: Apply fungicides containing propiconazole or azoxystrobin. Remove infected plant debris.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.prevention: Use resistant hybrids, practice crop rotation, and till infected residue.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: The corn leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.prevention: Monitor regularly for early pest and disease detection.

Grape___Black_rot.description: Grape black rot causes brown circular lesions with dark borders on leaves and shrivelled black mummified berries. It can destroy entire crops in wet seasons.
Grape___Black_rot.treatment: Apply fungicides containing myclobutanil or captan from bud break through veraison.
Grape___Black_rot.prevention: Remove mummified berries, prune for air circulation, and apply protective sprays early in the season.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: Esca or Black Measles causes interveinal chlorosis and necrosis on leaves with a tiger-stripe pattern. It is a wood disease that progresses slowly but kills vines.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).cause: Fungal complex - Phaeomoniella chlamydospora and others
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).treatment: No effective chemical treatment. Remove and destroy infected wood. Protect pruning wounds with fungicide paste.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).prevention: Avoid large pruning wounds, apply wound protectants, and remove infected vines promptly.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: Leaf blight causes dark brown angular spots on grape leaves, leading to premature defoliation and reduced fruit quality.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides or mancozeb. Improve canopy management to reduce humidity.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.prevention: Prune for good air circulation and avoid excessive irrigation.

Grape___healthy.description: The grape leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Grape___healthy.prevention: Regular canopy management and monitoring for early disease detection.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: Citrus greening or HLB is one of the most devastating citrus diseases. It causes yellowing of leaves in a blotchy pattern, small lopsided fruit, and eventual tree death.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).cause: Bacterium - Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus, spread by Asian citrus psyllid
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).treatment: No cure exists. Remove and destroy infected trees. Control psyllid populations with insecticides.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).prevention: Use certified disease-free planting material, control psyllid vectors, and quarantine new plant material.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot causes water-soaked lesions on leaves that turn brown and fall out, creating a shot-hole appearance. It also causes sunken spots on fruit.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides during the growing season. Avoid overhead irrigation.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, avoid wounding trees, and maintain proper nutrition.

Peach___healthy.description: The peach leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Peach___healthy.prevention: Monitor regularly and maintain good orchard sanitation.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot on pepper causes water-soaked lesions on leaves and fruit that turn brown with yellow halos. Severe infections cause defoliation and fruit loss.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides combined with mancozeb. Remove infected plant material.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed, practice crop rotation, and avoid overhead irrigation.

Pepper,_bell___healthy.description: The pepper leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Pepper,_bell___healthy.prevention: Monitor regularly and ensure proper spacing for air circulation.

Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight causes dark brown concentric ring lesions on lower leaves, resembling a target pattern. It reduces photosynthesis and can significantly reduce yield.
Potato___Early_blight.treatment: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil or mancozeb. Remove infected lower leaves.
Potato___Early_blight.prevention: Rotate crops, use certified seed potatoes, and avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation.

Potato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is one of the most destructive potato diseases. It causes water-soaked grey-green lesions that turn brown and spread rapidly, destroying entire fields.
Potato___Late_blight.treatment: Apply fungicides containing metalaxyl or chlorothalonil immediately upon detection. Destroy infected plants.
Potato___Late_blight.prevention: Use resistant varieties, apply preventive fungicides in humid conditions, and destroy volunteer plants.

Potato___healthy.description: The potato leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Potato___healthy.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed potatoes and monitor regularly.

Raspberry___healthy.description: The raspberry leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Raspberry___healthy.prevention: Ensure good drainage and monitor for cane diseases regularly.

Soybean___healthy.description: The soybean leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Soybean___healthy.prevention: Practice crop rotation and monitor for early signs of soybean diseases.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on squash appears as white powdery patches on leaf surfaces. Severe infections cause leaves to yellow, wither, and die prematurely.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.treatment: Apply sulphur-based fungicides or potassium bicarbonate sprays. Remove heavily infected leaves.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Ensure good air circulation, avoid overhead watering, and plant resistant varieties.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Leaf scorch causes small dark purple spots on the upper leaf surface that enlarge and turn reddish-brown with purple borders. Severe infections cause leaf death.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.treatment: Apply fungicides containing captan or myclobutanil. Remove and destroy infected leaves.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation, ensure good plant spacing, and remove old infected foliage.

Strawberry___healthy.description: The strawberry leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Strawberry___healthy.prevention: Monitor regularly and maintain good bed sanitation by removing old leaves.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot causes small water-soaked lesions on tomato leaves and fruit that turn brown with yellow halos. It thrives in warm, wet conditions.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides combined with mancozeb. Remove infected plant debris.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use disease-free seed, practice crop rotation, and avoid working with plants when wet.

Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight on tomato causes dark brown concentric ring lesions with a yellow halo on older leaves. It progresses upward through the plant causing defoliation.
Tomato___Early_blight.treatment: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil or mancozeb. Remove infected lower leaves.
Tomato___Early_blight.prevention: Use mulch to prevent soil splash, rotate crops, and stake plants for air circulation.

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight causes irregular water-soaked grey-green lesions that turn brown. White mould may appear on the underside of leaves in humid conditions. It spreads extremely rapidly.
Tomato___Late_blight.treatment: Apply fungicides containing metalaxyl or chlorothalonil immediately. Remove and destroy all infected plant material.
Tomato___Late_blight.prevention: Use resistant varieties, apply preventive sprays during humid weather, and avoid overhead irrigation.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Leaf mould causes pale green to yellow spots on the upper leaf surface with olive-green to grey velvety mould on the underside. It thrives in high humidity greenhouses.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.treatment: Improve ventilation, reduce humidity, and apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil or mancozeb.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.prevention: Ensure good greenhouse ventilation, avoid overhead watering, and plant resistant varieties.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot causes numerous small circular spots with dark borders and light grey centres on lower leaves. It causes severe defoliation starting from the bottom of the plant.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.treatment: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil or copper. Remove infected lower leaves immediately.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.prevention: Rotate crops, use mulch, avoid overhead watering, and stake plants for better air circulation.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Two-spotted spider mites cause stippling on leaves - tiny yellow or white dots - and fine webbing on the undersides of leaves. Severe infestations turn leaves bronze and cause defoliation.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.treatment: Apply miticides or insecticidal soap. Introduce predatory mites as biological control.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.prevention: Avoid dusty conditions, maintain plant moisture, and monitor the undersides of leaves regularly.

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot causes brown circular lesions with concentric rings and yellow halos on leaves, stems, and fruit. It can cause significant defoliation in warm humid conditions.
Tomato___Target_Spot.treatment: Apply fungicides containing azoxystrobin or chlorothalonil. Remove infected leaves.
Tomato___Target_Spot.prevention: Improve air circulation, avoid overhead irrigation, and rotate crops.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: TYLCV causes severe yellowing and upward curling of leaves, stunted growth, and flower drop. It is transmitted by whiteflies and can cause complete crop failure.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.cause: Virus - Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus, transmitted by Bemisia tabaci whitefly
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.treatment: No cure. Remove and destroy infected plants immediately. Control whitefly populations with insecticides.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.prevention: Use virus-resistant varieties, control whitefly populations, use reflective mulches, and use insect-proof screens in greenhouses.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato mosaic virus causes light and dark green mosaic patterns on leaves, leaf distortion, and stunted growth. It reduces fruit quality and yield significantly.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.cause: Virus - Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV), spread by contact and infected tools
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.treatment: No cure. Remove and destroy infected plants. Disinfect tools with bleach solution between uses.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.prevention: Use virus-free certified seed, disinfect tools, wash hands before handling plants, and control aphid vectors.

Tomato___healthy.description: The tomato leaf appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
Tomato___healthy.prevention: Monitor regularly, maintain proper spacing, and practice good garden hygiene.
